turns.content:
Gavin is non-speaking and cannot use words.
He makes sounds to express himself, including happy sounds, angry sounds, and singing-like sounds.
He uses an AAC device on an iPad with TouchChat.
He uses his AAC device to ask for help, for example when he cannot open his water bottle.
If he selects car, it means he wants to go for a ride.
If he selects iPad, it may mean the device is not working or he cannot find what he wants.
Checking his search history can help identify what he is trying to access.
If he selects a color, he is looking for an object of that color, and at home it usually means a mini bus toy.
He may approach, touch, or lead you to what he needs.
He may sit very close to show he wants attention.
turns.sectionTitle: What helps the day go well

turns.content:
He needs regular reminders to use the bathroom.
He does not independently communicate when he needs the bathroom.
The home has hourly prompts to remind him to use the bathroom.
He will usually go when prompted but may need additional reminders.
He urinates in the toilet but has bowel movements in a pull-up.
He often hides behind furniture or curtains and grunts when having a bowel movement.
He needs food often and needs frequent access to food.
Repeated trips to the fridge or grabbing cheese usually means he is hungry.
turns.sectionTitle: What helps the day go well

turns.content:
He enjoys iPad time, videos, mini bus toys, walks, and car rides.
He also likes going to IKEA and Bass Pro Shops in Foxborough.
Car rides help regulate him when he is upset.
Walks can help regulate him too, but only with at least two caregivers for safety.
Helping him find items on his iPad reduces frustration.
turns.sectionTitle: What helps the day go well

turns.content:
Not being able to access desired content on his iPad can upset him.
Being unable to open items because of low muscle tone can upset him.
Hunger or lack of available food can upset him.
Difficulty finding preferred items such as toys can upset him.
turns.sectionTitle: What can upset or overwhelm them
turns.stepTitle: What can upset or overwhelm them

turns.content:
Making loud or angry vocalizations can mean he needs help.
Running away or eloping is a sign he needs help.
Biting his hand when very dysregulated is a sign he needs help.
Hiding and grunting can mean he is having a bowel movement.
Pulling or leading a caregiver to something can mean he needs help.
Sitting very close or seeking extra attention can mean he needs help.

Offer a car ride to help calm him.
Help him access what he is trying to find on his iPad.
Redirect him instead of blocking behaviors.
Do not physically stop him from biting his hand, because he may bite you.
turns.sectionTitle: What helps when they are having a hard time
turns.stepTitle: What helps when they are having a hard time

turns.content:
Gavin has low muscle tone and may need help opening items.
He requires at least two caregivers for outings such as car rides or walks.
Elopement is a safety risk.
Hand biting is a safety risk to himself and to others if someone blocks it.
